list.name: Changes
list.comment: Summary We replaced the single legacy user repository pattern with role-specific repositories to improve clarity and prepare the backend for role-specific business logic. Roles created: customer, vendor, manager, admin. Implementation choice: keep the existing users collection to avoid an immediate DB migration; the new repositories enforce role filtering at read/write time. Files to add (examples): repositories/customers.repository.ts, repositories/vendors.repository.ts, repositories/managers.repository.ts, repositories/admins.repository.ts, services/customers.service.ts. Example API route added: app/api/customers/[id]/route.ts. Do NOT delete repositories/user.repository.ts until CI/build passes and no references remain. Why this change Domain clarity: separate repositories map to domain concepts (each role can develop its own business logic). Safer rollout: by wrapping the same users collection and only enforcing role at repository layer, we avoid migrating existing data immediately. Easier future evolution: later we can migrate specific roles to their own collections if required without changing service and route contracts. Design decisions and rationale Storage: reuse users collection. Avoid data migration for now. Each role gets a repository that is a small wrapper over the same collection and adds a role filter (e.g., { role: 'customer' }). Services mediate domain logic and call repositories. Auth service will route creation to the appropriate repository and use a role-agnostic lookup helper for login. Keep DTOs in dto/user.dto.ts and types in types/users.type.ts — ensure toSafeUser removes password and other sensitive data. Repository contract (per-role) Path pattern: repositories/<role>.repository.ts (e.g., repositories/customers.repository.ts) Methods: findById(id: string): Promise<UserDocument | null> findByEmail(email: string): Promise<UserDocument | null> create(payload: CreateUserPayload): Promise<userdocument></userdocument> update(id: string, updates: Partial<createuserpayload>): Promise<UserDocument | null></createuserpayload> delete(id: string): Promise<boolean></boolean> findAll(filter?: Partial<userfilter>): Promise<UserDocument[]></userfilter> Implementation notes: Use getDb() from lib/mongodb.ts. All queries include { role: '<role>' } filter. Insert should set role, createdAt, updatedAt. Service contract (per-role) Path pattern: services/<role>.service.ts (e.g., services/customers.service.ts) Methods: getById(id: string) list(filter, paging) create(data) update(id, data) Responsibilities: Validate inputs, map DTOs, call repository, remove sensitive fields before returning. Auth flow changes Registration: services/auth.service.ts will pick the repository based on the incoming role and call create there. Password hashing (bcrypt) and session creation remain in auth service. Login: Add helper findUserByEmailAcrossRoles(email) that checks role repositories in order (customers → vendors → managers → admins) and returns the first user found. Compare hashed password and create a session as before. This keeps auth centralized but rolespecific creation and user lookup in repositories. Example API route behavior (GET customer by id) Endpoint: GET /api/customers/:id — file app/api/customers/[id]/route.ts Behavior: Validate id param. Call services/customers.service.ts -> getById(id). If not found: return 404. Strip sensitive fields (e.g., password) before sending JSON 200.

task.summary: feat(auth): implement secure user signup with roles and server actions

changelist.name: Changes
changelist.comment: feat(auth): implement secure user signup with roles and server actions

task.summary: fixed the layout to manage jwt token and update session handling + removed unused code and updated imports + fixed the header
fixed the layout to manage jwt token and update session handling + removed unused code and updated imports + fixed the header

Summary We replaced the single legacy user repository pattern with role-specific repositories to improve clarity and prepare the backend for role-specific business logic. Roles created: customer, vendor, manager, admin. Implementation choice: keep the existing users collection to avoid an immediate DB migration; the new repositories enforce role filtering at read/write time. Files to add (examples): repositories/customers.repository.ts, repositories/vendors.repository.ts, repositories/managers.repository.ts, repositories/admins.repository.ts, services/customers.service.ts. Example API route added: app/api/customers/[id]/route.ts. Do NOT delete repositories/user.repository.ts until CI/build passes and no references remain. Why this change Domain clarity: separate repositories map to domain concepts (each role can develop its own business logic). Safer rollout: by wrapping the same users collection and only enforcing role at repository layer, we avoid migrating existing data immediately. Easier future evolution: later we can migrate specific roles to their own collections if required without changing service and route contracts. Design decisions and rationale Storage: reuse users collection. Avoid data migration for now. Each role gets a repository that is a small wrapper over the same collection and adds a role filter (e.g., { role: 'customer' }). Services mediate domain logic and call repositories. Auth service will route creation to the appropriate repository and use a role-agnostic lookup helper for login. Keep DTOs in dto/user.dto.ts and types in types/users.type.ts — ensure toSafeUser removes password and other sensitive data. Repository contract (per-role) Path pattern: repositories/<role>.repository.ts (e.g., repositories/customers.repository.ts) Methods: findById(id: string): Promise<UserDocument | null> findByEmail(email: string): Promise<UserDocument | null> create(payload: CreateUserPayload): Promise<userdocument></userdocument> update(id: string, updates: Partial<createuserpayload>): Promise<UserDocument | null></createuserpayload> delete(id: string): Promise<boolean></boolean> findAll(filter?: Partial<userfilter>): Promise<UserDocument[]></userfilter> Implementation notes: Use getDb() from lib/mongodb.ts. All queries include { role: '<role>' } filter. Insert should set role, createdAt, updatedAt. Service contract (per-role) Path pattern: services/<role>.service.ts (e.g., services/customers.service.ts) Methods: getById(id: string) list(filter, paging) create(data) update(id, data) Responsibilities: Validate inputs, map DTOs, call repository, remove sensitive fields before returning. Auth flow changes Registration: services/auth.service.ts will pick the repository based on the incoming role and call create there. Password hashing (bcrypt) and session creation remain in auth service. Login: Add helper findUserByEmailAcrossRoles(email) that checks role repositories in order (customers → vendors → managers → admins) and returns the first user found. Compare hashed password and create a session as before. This keeps auth centralized but rolespecific creation and user lookup in repositories. Example API route behavior (GET customer by id) Endpoint: GET /api/customers/:id — file app/api/customers/[id]/route.ts Behavior: Validate id param. Call services/customers.service.ts -> getById(id). If not found: return 404. Strip sensitive fields (e.g., password) before sending JSON 200.

MESSAGE.value: complete technician profile with name/image updates and field restrictions
feat(auth): implement secure user signup with roles and server actions
fixed the layout to manage jwt token and update session handling + removed unused code and updated imports + fixed the header

Summary We replaced the single legacy user repository pattern with role-specific repositories to improve clarity and prepare the backend for role-specific business logic. Roles created: customer, vendor, manager, admin. Implementation choice: keep the existing users collection to avoid an immediate DB migration; the new repositories enforce role filtering at read/write time. Files to add (examples): repositories/customers.repository.ts, repositories/vendors.repository.ts, repositories/managers.repository.ts, repositories/admins.repository.ts, services/customers.service.ts. Example API route added: app/api/customers/[id]/route.ts. Do NOT delete repositories/user.repository.ts until CI/build passes and no references remain. Why this change Domain clarity: separate repositories map to domain concepts (each role can develop its own business logic). Safer rollout: by wrapping the same users collection and only enforcing role at repository layer, we avoid migrating existing data immediately. Easier future evolution: later we can migrate specific roles to their own collections if required without changing service and route contracts. Design decisions and rationale Storage: reuse users collection. Avoid data migration for now. Each role gets a repository that is a small wrapper over the same collection and adds a role filter (e.g., { role: 'customer' }). Services mediate domain logic and call repositories. Auth service will route creation to the appropriate repository and use a role-agnostic lookup helper for login. Keep DTOs in dto/user.dto.ts and types in types/users.type.ts — ensure toSafeUser removes password and other sensitive data. Repository contract (per-role) Path pattern: repositories/<role>.repository.ts (e.g., repositories/customers.repository.ts) Methods: findById(id: string): Promise<UserDocument | null> findByEmail(email: string): Promise<UserDocument | null> create(payload: CreateUserPayload): Promise<userdocument></userdocument> update(id: string, updates: Partial<createuserpayload>): Promise<UserDocument | null></createuserpayload> delete(id: string): Promise<boolean></boolean> findAll(filter?: Partial<userfilter>): Promise<UserDocument[]></userfilter> Implementation notes: Use getDb() from lib/mongodb.ts. All queries include { role: '<role>' } filter. Insert should set role, createdAt, updatedAt. Service contract (per-role) Path pattern: services/<role>.service.ts (e.g., services/customers.service.ts) Methods: getById(id: string) list(filter, paging) create(data) update(id, data) Responsibilities: Validate inputs, map DTOs, call repository, remove sensitive fields before returning. Auth flow changes Registration: services/auth.service.ts will pick the repository based on the incoming role and call create there. Password hashing (bcrypt) and session creation remain in auth service. Login: Add helper findUserByEmailAcrossRoles(email) that checks role repositories in order (customers → vendors → managers → admins) and returns the first user found. Compare hashed password and create a session as before. This keeps auth centralized but rolespecific creation and user lookup in repositories. Example API route behavior (GET customer by id) Endpoint: GET /api/customers/:id — file app/api/customers/[id]/route.ts Behavior: Validate id param. Call services/customers.service.ts -> getById(id). If not found: return 404. Strip sensitive fields (e.g., password) before sending JSON 200.

option.name: LAST_COMMIT_MESSAGE
option.value: Summary We replaced the single legacy user repository pattern with role-specific repositories to improve clarity and prepare the backend for role-specific business logic. Roles created: customer, vendor, manager, admin. Implementation choice: keep the existing users collection to avoid an immediate DB migration; the new repositories enforce role filtering at read/write time. Files to add (examples): repositories/customers.repository.ts, repositories/vendors.repository.ts, repositories/managers.repository.ts, repositories/admins.repository.ts, services/customers.service.ts. Example API route added: app/api/customers/[id]/route.ts. Do NOT delete repositories/user.repository.ts until CI/build passes and no references remain. Why this change Domain clarity: separate repositories map to domain concepts (each role can develop its own business logic). Safer rollout: by wrapping the same users collection and only enforcing role at repository layer, we avoid migrating existing data immediately. Easier future evolution: later we can migrate specific roles to their own collections if required without changing service and route contracts. Design decisions and rationale Storage: reuse users collection. Avoid data migration for now. Each role gets a repository that is a small wrapper over the same collection and adds a role filter (e.g., { role: 'customer' }). Services mediate domain logic and call repositories. Auth service will route creation to the appropriate repository and use a role-agnostic lookup helper for login. Keep DTOs in dto/user.dto.ts and types in types/users.type.ts — ensure toSafeUser removes password and other sensitive data. Repository contract (per-role) Path pattern: repositories/<role>.repository.ts (e.g., repositories/customers.repository.ts) Methods: findById(id: string): Promise<UserDocument | null> findByEmail(email: string): Promise<UserDocument | null> create(payload: CreateUserPayload): Promise<userdocument></userdocument> update(id: string, updates: Partial<createuserpayload>): Promise<UserDocument | null></createuserpayload> delete(id: string): Promise<boolean></boolean> findAll(filter?: Partial<userfilter>): Promise<UserDocument[]></userfilter> Implementation notes: Use getDb() from lib/mongodb.ts. All queries include { role: '<role>' } filter. Insert should set role, createdAt, updatedAt. Service contract (per-role) Path pattern: services/<role>.service.ts (e.g., services/customers.service.ts) Methods: getById(id: string) list(filter, paging) create(data) update(id, data) Responsibilities: Validate inputs, map DTOs, call repository, remove sensitive fields before returning. Auth flow changes Registration: services/auth.service.ts will pick the repository based on the incoming role and call create there. Password hashing (bcrypt) and session creation remain in auth service. Login: Add helper findUserByEmailAcrossRoles(email) that checks role repositories in order (customers → vendors → managers → admins) and returns the first user found. Compare hashed password and create a session as before. This keeps auth centralized but rolespecific creation and user lookup in repositories. Example API route behavior (GET customer by id) Endpoint: GET /api/customers/:id — file app/api/customers/[id]/route.ts Behavior: Validate id param. Call services/customers.service.ts -> getById(id). If not found: return 404. Strip sensitive fields (e.g., password) before sending JSON 200.
